title: Dog Shedding Control Guide (2026) – Reduce Shedding by 80%
seoTitle: Dog Shedding Control Guide 2026 – Reduce Shedding Fast | GetPawsy
seoDescription: Proven methods to reduce dog shedding by up to 80%. Deshedding tools, grooming routines, diet tips and breed-specific shedding management.
excerpt: Proven methods to reduce dog shedding by up to 80% — deshedding tools, grooming routines, diet tips and breed-specific management plans.

sections.heading: Understanding Why Dogs Shed
sections.content:
All dogs shed — it's a natural process of replacing old or damaged hair with new growth. But the amount of shedding varies dramatically by breed, season, health and diet.

**Double-coated breeds** (Huskies, German Shepherds, Golden Retrievers) shed the most, especially during seasonal 'coat blows' in spring and fall when they drop their undercoat.

**Single-coated breeds** (Poodles, Bichon Frise, Maltese) shed minimally but require regular haircuts since their hair grows continuously.

**Short-coated breeds** (Beagles, Bulldogs, Labradors) shed moderately year-round with less dramatic seasonal changes.

You can't stop shedding entirely, but you can reduce loose hair in your home by 60–80% with the right grooming routine and tools.

For the complete grooming toolkit, see our [dog grooming essentials guide](/guides/dog-grooming-essentials) and browse our [dog grooming kits collection](/collections/best-dog-grooming-kits).

sections.heading: Best Deshedding Tools
sections.content:
**Undercoat rake**: The most versatile deshedding tool. Wide-spaced teeth reach through the topcoat to remove loose undercoat without cutting or damaging healthy hair. Best for double-coated breeds.

**Deshedding blade**: A curved metal blade that strips loose hair efficiently. Use with light pressure — the blade does the work. Excellent for heavy shedding season.

**Rubber curry brush**: Attracts and traps loose hair with static. Works well on short coats and as a pre-bath tool. Dogs typically enjoy the massage sensation.

**High-velocity dryer**: Professional groomers' secret weapon. Blasts loose hair out of the coat faster than any brush. A 15-minute session can remove as much loose hair as an hour of brushing.

For breed-specific tool recommendations, see our [best dog brushes by coat type guide](/guides/best-dog-brushes-by-coat-type). For all grooming tools, browse the [dog grooming tools guide](/guides/dog-grooming-tools-guide).

sections.heading: The Optimal Deshedding Routine
sections.content:
**Weekly maintenance** (non-shedding season):
- Brush with a slicker brush for 10–15 minutes, 2–3 times per week
- Focus on areas where loose hair accumulates: behind ears, chest, haunches, tail base

**Shedding season routine** (spring and fall, 2–4 weeks):
- Daily 15-minute brushing sessions with an undercoat rake
- Weekly bath with a deshedding shampoo and conditioner
- Follow bath with a thorough blow-dry (low heat) to release remaining loose hair
- Use a deshedding blade for final pass after drying

**Bath frequency during shedding season**: Increase to weekly baths during heavy shedding. The warm water and massage loosen dead hair that brushing alone can't reach. Use a [quality dog shampoo](/guides/best-dog-shampoo-guide) formulated for deshedding.

For detailed grooming schedules, see our [how often to groom your dog guide](/guides/how-often-groom-dog).

sections.heading: Diet and Supplements for Coat Health
sections.content:
What your dog eats directly impacts coat quality and shedding volume.

**Omega-3 fatty acids**: The single most effective dietary supplement for reducing shedding. Fish oil supplements improve coat quality, reduce inflammation and decrease loose hair by 20–30%.

**Protein quality**: Hair is 95% protein (keratin). A diet with high-quality animal protein supports healthy hair growth and reduces excessive shedding from poor nutrition.

**Hydration**: Dehydrated dogs have dry, brittle coats that shed more. Ensure fresh water is always available. Consider wet food or adding water to kibble.

**Foods to include**: Salmon, sardines, eggs, coconut oil (small amounts), sweet potatoes.

**Foods to avoid**: Cheap fillers (corn, soy), artificial colors and preservatives that can trigger skin reactions and increased shedding.

**When to see a vet**: Sudden excessive shedding, bald patches, or flaky skin can indicate allergies, thyroid issues or other health problems.

sections.heading: Managing Shedding in Your Home
sections.content:
Even with excellent grooming, some loose hair will end up on your furniture and floors. Here's how to manage it:

**Robot vacuum**: Run daily during shedding season. Consistent pickup prevents hair from embedding in carpets and upholstery.

**Lint rollers**: Keep one by the door, one in the car and one at your desk. Quick passes before leaving the house maintain a presentable appearance.

**Washable furniture covers**: Easier to clean than upholstery. Toss in the washing machine weekly.

**Air purifier with HEPA filter**: Captures airborne dander and loose hair particles. Especially helpful for family members with pet allergies.

**Designate dog-free zones**: One room where no loose hair accumulates gives you a respite. Use baby gates or closed doors.

For the complete grooming toolkit, browse our [dog grooming kits collection](/collections/best-dog-grooming-kits).

sections.heading: Breed-Specific Shedding Management
sections.content:
**Heavy shedders** (Huskies, German Shepherds, Akitas): Daily brushing during coat blows. Invest in an undercoat rake and high-velocity dryer. Expect 2–4 weeks of intense shedding twice yearly.

**Moderate shedders** (Labradors, Beagles, Corgis): 2–3 brushing sessions per week year-round. A rubber curry brush and slicker brush handle most needs.

**Light shedders** (Poodles, Yorkies, Maltese): Minimal shedding but require regular haircuts every 6–8 weeks. Daily brushing prevents mats and tangles.

**Non-shedding myth**: No dog is truly hypoallergenic or non-shedding. Some breeds shed less and produce less dander, but all dogs release some hair and allergens.

For nail care as part of your grooming routine, see our [dog nail trimming guide](/guides/dog-nail-trimming-guide).

faq.question: How can I reduce my dog's shedding?
faq.answer: Regular brushing (2–3 times weekly), omega-3 supplements, quality diet, deshedding baths during shedding season and proper deshedding tools can reduce loose hair by 60–80%.

faq.question: What is the best tool for dog shedding?
faq.answer: An undercoat rake is the most effective tool for double-coated breeds. For short coats, a rubber curry brush works best. A high-velocity dryer is the professional-grade option.

faq.question: Why is my dog shedding so much?
faq.answer: Heavy shedding is normal during spring and fall seasonal coat changes. Year-round excessive shedding may indicate poor diet, allergies, stress or an underlying health condition.

faq.question: Can diet help with dog shedding?
faq.answer: Yes. Omega-3 fatty acid supplements and high-quality protein diets significantly improve coat health and reduce excessive shedding.
